title: Grangea cuneifolia
taxonomy: Grangea cuneifolia belongs to the family Asteraceae, commonly known as the daisy or sunflower family. This classification places it within the group of flowering plants known as angiosperms. The plant's classification is further defined by its order, which is Asterales. This order is comprised of flowering plants that share similarities in their flower structure. Grangea cuneifolia is a species of flowering plant native to the high-altitude regions of the Himalayas.
uses: Grangea cuneifolia is used in traditional medicine, particularly in the Himalayan region. Its leaves are believed to have antiseptic and anti-inflammatory properties, which can help treat various health issues. The plant is also used to treat fever and other infections. Additionally, Grangea cuneifolia is believed to possess antioxidant properties, which can help protect against cell damage and oxidative stress. More research is needed to fully understand the medicinal uses and potential benefits of Grangea cuneifolia.

properties: Grangea cuneifolia is known to possess therapeutic properties, primarily due to its ability to inhibit the growth of bacteria and reduce inflammation. intro: Grangea cuneifolia, also known as Grangea millefolia or Indian snakeroot, is a plant species in the family Asteraceae, characterized by its fern-like foliage and small white flowers. The health benefits of Grangea cuneifolia include its use in traditional medicine to treat fever, rheumatism, and respiratory issues, such as bronchitis and asthma, due to its anti-inflammatory and expectorant properties. The bioactive constituents of Grangea cuneifolia include flavonoids, terpenoids, and phenolic acids, which contribute to its medicinal properties. In traditional Ayurvedic medicine, Grangea cuneifolia is often made into various herbal preparations, including teas, infusions, and decoctions, which are used to promote overall health and well-being.
